mnemonics.title: Flow Properties
mnemonics.content: CAF: Capacity constraint (0≤f≤c), Augmenting path exists until no path, Flow is max when no augmenting path exists

mnemonics.title: Ford-Fulkerson Steps
mnemonics.content: FIND bottleneck, INCREASE flow along path, UPDATE residual graph (FIFO: Find, Increase, Update)

mnemonics.title: Min Cut Finding
mnemonics.content: REACH from source in residual (BFS/DFS) - what you REACH is S, what you DON'T is T

acronyms.usage: Basic max flow algorithm using augmenting paths
Partition where source in S, sink in T

visualTips.visualization: Think of it as 'remaining capacity map'. Forward edges show how much more can flow, backward edges show how much can be cancelled.
It's a 'flow pipeline' from source to sink. Bottleneck is the narrowest pipe that limits how much can push through.
Imagine cutting the network with scissors - sum up capacities of all edges you cut. The minimum cut is the cheapest place to break the network.
Think of network as water pipes. Maximum water flow equals the capacity of the weakest bottleneck (minimum cut).

analogies.analogy: Like a highway system with capacity limits on each road. Cars (flow) travel from city A (source) to city B (sink).
Like GPS showing remaining route options. Forward edges are untraveled roads, backward edges are roads where you can reverse direction.
The narrowest straw in a drink - determines maximum flow rate regardless of other straws' width.
A completely full highway - no more cars can enter from that direction.
The weakest link in a chain - break here and source gets disconnected from sink with minimum effort.
Finding an alternative delivery route when current route is congested.
